label: fiction
title: The Purple Label
setting: A meal-preparation contamination trial in 2026, where a personal grudge competes with a packaging printer that reused yesterday’s allergen label.
charge: Intentionally supplying a falsely labelled allergen meal and causing serious injury
elements: The meal supplied to Orin Kale as allergen-free contained ground nut paste and caused his serious injury.
Rina Dovel deliberately added the nut paste or knowingly caused the false allergen-free label to be applied.
Rina intended to cause serious injury or knew that serious injury was a probable consequence.
hook: A purple label promises an allergen-safe meal. Inside is ground nut paste—and the supervisor signed the shift after threatening the customer.

accused.human: Rina Dovel runs free weekend cooking classes and supports a child with severe allergies at home.
accused.if_guilty: A conviction means prison, loss of food-safety work, and public branding as someone who weaponised the danger she understands personally.

prosecution.speaker: pc
prosecution.text: Rina Dovel knew Orin Kale’s severe nut allergy. The night before his delivery, she told him he would regret humiliating her. She supervised the kitchen, moved ingredient tubs beside the packing line and signed the dispatch sheet for the meal that nearly killed him. The prosecution says the purple safety label was not an accident; it was how Rina concealed a deliberate attack.

defence.speaker: dc
defence.text: Rina’s message was cruel, but it said nothing about food or allergies. At 11:42, the label printer disconnected and resumed an old queue. Four meals packed in the next minute carried the wrong labels, including ordinary meals marked safe and a safe meal marked as containing nuts. The defence says a documented equipment failure—not an attempted poisoning—put the purple label on Orin’s tray.

prosecution.speaker: pc
prosecution.text: The printer fault does not erase Rina’s motive or access. She knew Orin’s allergy, threatened him hours before production, moved the nut paste beside the line and signed the dispatch. The camera has blind spots, and a general batch error could give cover to one deliberate substitution. If the surrounding mismatches do not reasonably explain Orin’s particular tray, the connected evidence identifies Rina and proves intent.

defence.speaker: dc
defence.text: The prosecution asks you to isolate Orin’s meal from the three other errors produced beside it. The printer log supplies the exact failure, time and mechanism. The surviving trays show labels switched in both directions, which makes no sense as a targeted attack. No camera shows Rina touching Orin’s sealed tray, and her signature covered six production lines. Anger creates suspicion; it cannot exclude the documented accident. The lawful verdict is not guilty.

epilogue: Orin recovered slowly and accepted Rina’s apology for the message, not for poisoning him. The kitchen replaced the printer, introduced scan-to-pack checks and recalled every meal from the affected batch. Rina returned after additional safety training and later helped Orin campaign for mandatory label verification in commercial kitchens. The incident remained painful for both.

beats.speaker: w1
beats.text: I’m Bela Neris, the food-safety investigator. Laboratory testing found ground nut paste throughout Orin’s sealed meal, although its purple label identified the tray as allergen-free. Rina signed the shift sheet covering packing and dispatch across six production lines. Her signature made her responsible for the batch, but it did not record who prepared or labelled this individual tray.
beats.reveal_note: The contamination and supervisory signature look incriminating, but the shift sheet does not identify who handled Orin’s tray.

beats.speaker: w2
beats.text: I’m Orin Kale. Rina and I had been close friends until an argument about her cooking classes. The night before my delivery, she messaged that I would regret humiliating her. The next day, I checked the purple allergen-safe label and ate three bites. My throat closed, I lost consciousness and spent four days in hospital. I believed the message and meal were connected.
beats.reveal_note: Orin establishes the serious injury and a possible motive, while making clear that his identification of Rina is an inference.

beats.speaker: w1
beats.text: Your first report described Orin’s label as deliberate? Yes. That was before we found the other three meals. Those trays had labels wrong in both directions? Yes. Two ordinary meals were marked allergen-free, and one safe meal was marked as containing nuts. That pattern was not consistent with targeting Orin alone, so I withdrew my conclusion that his tray had been deliberately relabelled.

turns.speaker: dc
turns.text: Your first report described Orin’s label as deliberate?

turns.speaker: w1
turns.text: Yes. That was before we found the other three meals.

turns.speaker: dc
turns.text: Those trays had labels wrong in both directions?

turns.speaker: w1
turns.text: Yes. Two ordinary meals were marked allergen-free, and one safe meal was marked as containing nuts. That pattern was not consistent with targeting Orin alone, so I withdrew my conclusion that his tray had been deliberately relabelled.

beats.reveal_note: The investigator’s revised conclusion places Orin’s tray inside a wider two-way labelling failure.

beats.speaker: pc
beats.text: The kitchen camera shows Rina moving sealed ingredient tubs to the preparation bench at 11:35. Ground nut paste was among them because ordinary meals using it were scheduled next. The footage covers the bench and dispatch table but has a blind corner beside the printer. It never shows Rina opening Orin’s tray or applying a label.
beats.reveal_note: The footage confirms access to the ingredient and packing area, while providing no direct image of tampering.

beats.speaker: dc
beats.text: The printer log records a network loss at 11:42 and reconnection forty-one seconds later. Its local queue then resumed at job 804 in the previous day’s label sequence instead of the current server sequence. Orin’s tray and three surviving meals were labelled between 11:43:02 and 11:43:49, before the central server corrected the queue.
beats.reveal_note: The printer log gives an exact mechanism and time window for the wrong label on Orin’s meal.

beats.speaker: w3
beats.text: I’m Sava Denn, the technician operating the packaging station that day. I inspected three surviving meals from the same minute. Two ordinary meals containing nut paste carried purple allergen-free labels, while one genuinely safe meal carried a nut warning. The tray codes match the order in the resumed printer queue. I found no sign that any label had been peeled off or replaced by hand.
beats.reveal_note: The surviving meals reproduce the printer fault in both directions and show no physical evidence of hand relabelling.

beats.speaker: w4
beats.text: I’m Rina Dovel. I signed the sheet because I supervised six production lines. I moved the sealed ingredient tubs when the preparation team asked for the next recipe, then returned to the cooking area. I never opened Orin’s tray or used the label printer. My message was angry and shameful, but I did not mention food, his allergy or any plan to hurt him.
beats.reveal_note: Rina admits the threat and her access while denying the specific acts required for intentional contamination.

beats.speaker: w2
beats.text: You believed Rina’s message referred to the meal? I did because it arrived the night before. She never mentioned food, labels or your allergy? No. She said I would regret humiliating her. Had she ever ignored your allergy when cooking for you? Never. She had always been extremely careful with food around me.

turns.speaker: dc
turns.text: You believed Rina’s message referred to the meal?

turns.speaker: w2
turns.text: I did because it arrived the night before.

turns.speaker: dc
turns.text: She never mentioned food, labels or your allergy?

turns.speaker: w2
turns.text: No. She said I would regret humiliating her.

turns.speaker: dc
turns.text: Had she ever ignored your allergy when cooking for you?

turns.speaker: w2
turns.text: Never. She had always been extremely careful with food around me.

beats.reveal_note: Cross-examination limits the threat to evidence of anger and contrasts it with Rina’s history of allergy care.

beats.speaker: w3
beats.text: You saw the printer disconnect? Yes. The panel returned to green after forty-one seconds. You did not clear its local queue? No. The operating manual said a green connection meant the central queue had resumed. It did not warn that an old local job could restart. I followed the normal restart procedure, and nothing on the panel showed that yesterday’s labels were printing.

turns.speaker: w3
turns.text: Yes. The panel returned to green after forty-one seconds.

turns.speaker: pc
turns.text: You did not clear its local queue?

turns.speaker: w3
turns.text: No. The operating manual said a green connection meant the central queue had resumed. It did not warn that an old local job could restart. I followed the normal restart procedure, and nothing on the panel showed that yesterday’s labels were printing.

beats.reveal_note: Sava’s cross confirms that the warning was seen but the approved restart procedure concealed the stale queue.

beats.speaker: judge
beats.text: To convict, you must be sure that Rina deliberately added the nut paste or knowingly applied the false label, intending serious harm or knowing it was probable. Her anger, access and supervisory signature may support that inference, but none proves the act alone. Consider whether the printer log and other mismatched meals provide a reasonable accidental explanation. Rina does not have to prove exactly how the error occurred.
beats.reveal_note: The final direction separates motive and opportunity from proof of deliberate tampering and intent to harm.

twist: The purple label was not unique to Orin’s tray. After a forty-one-second network loss, the printer resumed yesterday’s queue and mislabelled four meals in both directions. Rina’s threat explained suspicion, but the surviving trays explained the injury.

jurors.label: Juror 2
jurors.persona: A blunt juror who challenges convenient memory from witnesses on both sides.

agree: Juror 2: Rina’s message was vicious, and she had access. That makes her suspicious; it does not put her hand on the tray.
Juror 2: Her signature covered six lines. Stop describing it as if she personally signed Orin’s label.
pushback: Juror 2: If this was targeted, explain the other three wrong meals. They do not disappear because the message sounds bad.
Juror 2: Bela withdrew the deliberate-label conclusion. I am not going to rely on a finding the investigator no longer supports.
concede: Juror 2: I accept that the printer failure is documented, not an excuse invented by Rina after the fact.
final: Juror 2: The threat proves anger. The printer log and surviving trays explain the label. I cannot convict Rina of an intentional attack.

jurors.label: Juror 3
jurors.persona: A cautious holdout who wants the decisive record stated without exaggeration.

agree: Juror 3: The log proves the network drop and stale job. It does not itself prove which label landed on Orin’s tray.
Juror 3: The surviving tray codes match the resumed queue. That is the independent check I needed before relying on the technician.
pushback: Juror 3: I am not dismissing motive. Rina made the threat hours before a meal containing the one ingredient Orin feared.
Juror 3: Four errors in one minute strongly suggest a fault, but could one deliberate substitution be hidden among them?
concede: Juror 3: I gave the shift signature too much weight. It establishes Rina’s role, not who handled this tray.
final: Juror 3: The prosecution theory is possible, but the verified printer sequence is reasonably possible too. That means not guilty.

jurors.label: Juror 4
jurors.persona: An empathetic juror who notices harm but refuses to let sympathy replace proof.

agree: Juror 4: Orin trusted that purple label and nearly died. His injury is certain, even if responsibility is not.
pushback: Juror 4: I believe Orin when he says the message frightened him. His belief about what Rina intended is still not direct evidence.
Juror 4: Rina’s apology for the message does not amount to an apology for poisoning him.
concede: Juror 4: The other unsafe labels could have hurt more people. They make this look like a catastrophic system failure, not a personal attack.
holdout: Juror 4: I am holding until we separate our anger at Rina’s message from proof that she touched the meal.
Juror 4: Orin deserves an answer, but a guilty verdict is not compensation for an industrial accident.
final: Juror 4: The evidence proves why Orin suspected Rina and why he was injured. It does not prove she intended either result.

jurors.label: Juror 5
jurors.persona: A systems-minded juror alert to both automation errors and deliberate manipulation.

agree: Juror 5: The system log, tray codes and physical labels agree. Three separate data points reproduce the stale-queue failure.
pushback: Juror 5: Could someone manually trigger an old job to disguise tampering? The log should distinguish a reconnection from an operator command.
Juror 5: The absence of peel marks matters. A hand-applied replacement usually disturbs the original adhesive.
concede: Juror 5: I accept the printer did exactly what the defence describes at 11:42. That mechanism accounts for all four mismatches.
burden_correct: Juror 5: Rina does not have to demonstrate every packet through the machine. The prosecution must exclude the documented fault.
Juror 5: A theory that Rina exploited the outage is possible, but possibility does not prove a deliberate act.
final: Juror 5: The fault is timestamped, repeatable and visible in three other trays. The prosecution has not connected Rina to intentional relabelling.

jurors.label: Juror 6
jurors.persona: A quiet questioner who tests what the strongest theory still leaves unexplained.

agree: Juror 6: If Rina planned this, how did she know the printer would disconnect at exactly the right moment?
Juror 6: If she applied the label by hand, why are there no peel marks and why do the tray codes follow the stale queue?
pushback: Juror 6: The camera has a blind corner. Could Rina have handled the tray there without leaving a trace?
Juror 6: Why did the contaminated meal reach Orin rather than another customer? Was his name visible before the label was applied?
concede: Juror 6: The three surviving errors answer my main question: Orin’s label was part of a sequence, not an isolated substitution.
final: Juror 6: The prosecution cannot explain the wider pattern under its targeted-attack theory. That unanswered question is reasonable doubt.

jurors.label: Juror 7
jurors.persona: A steady foreperson who makes the room distinguish suspicion from proof.

agree: Juror 7: We agree on the injury, the threat and the printer failure. The disputed questions are deliberate action and intent.
Juror 7: Let us test the prosecution theory against all four meals, not discuss Orin’s tray as though it was the only error.
pushback: Juror 7: Motive and opportunity are supporting facts. What evidence proves the act itself?
Juror 7: We should not use Rina’s ugly words to fill the camera’s blind corner with an event nobody saw.
concede: Juror 7: I accept that Rina’s shift signature was routine supervision across six lines, not a personal certification of Orin’s meal.
Juror 7: The printer evidence gives us more than a vague alternative. It gives us a complete accidental mechanism.
final: Juror 7: Suspicion is strong, but the deliberate act is not proved beyond reasonable doubt. My verdict is not guilty.

jurors.label: Juror 8
jurors.persona: A careful note-taker who separates a striking clue from the chain needed to prove identity.

agree: Juror 8: In my notes, the message supports motive and the signature supports opportunity. Neither identifies an act of tampering.
Juror 8: The camera shows Rina moving sealed tubs at 11:35, seven minutes before the printer fault. It does not show contact with Orin’s tray.
pushback: Juror 8: I need evidence beside each element: false meal, deliberate act and intent. We have a blank beside the second.
Juror 8: Am I counting the threat twice—once as motive and again as proof that she handled the label?
concede: Juror 8: I have moved the shift sheet out of the identity column. It proves responsibility for the batch, not criminal conduct.
final: Juror 8: My notes support an accidental label failure more strongly than a deliberate substitution. The required act and intent are not proved.

jurors.label: Juror 9
jurors.persona: A practical juror who tests digital records against ordinary workplace habits.

agree: Juror 9: A green panel normally tells a worker the machine recovered. Sava followed the manual and had no reason to clear a hidden queue.
Juror 9: Supervisors move ingredients and sign batch sheets. Those ordinary tasks should not become evidence of poisoning just because an accident followed.
pushback: Juror 9: Did anyone at the kitchen know this printer could restart yesterday’s queue? If Rina knew, the warning might matter differently.
Juror 9: The kitchen’s procedures failed Orin badly. We still have to decide whether Rina committed a crime rather than a workplace error.
concede: Juror 9: The operating manual confirms Sava’s account. The staff were not trained to detect this fault after the panel returned to green.
final: Juror 9: This was a preventable workplace disaster, but the evidence does not show Rina caused it deliberately.

jurors.label: Juror 10
jurors.persona: A sceptic who distrusts polished system evidence until its collection method is clear.

agree: Juror 10: The prosecution facts are simple and human: threat, access, ingredient and injury. The technical account needs careful testing.
pushback: Juror 10: Who preserved the printer log, and could anyone alter a queue record after Orin became ill?
burden_drift: Juror 10: Rina sent the threat. I keep wanting her to prove it had nothing to do with the meal.
Juror 10: Rina was the supervisor, so part of me expects her to explain every gap in the packing process.
concede: Juror 10: The tray codes independently match the queue record, and the labels show no hand replacement. I accept the technical evidence.
burden_correct: Juror 10: Rina does not have to prove her anger was harmless. The prosecution has to prove that she acted on it through this meal.
final: Juror 10: Once I stop making Rina disprove the accusation, the documented printer fault leaves a reasonable doubt.

jurors.label: Juror 11
jurors.persona: A rule-focused juror who brings the room back to the prosecution burden.

agree: Juror 11: The printer fault does not have to be certain. If it is reasonably possible, the prosecution has not proved a deliberate act.
Juror 11: We must be sure Rina knowingly caused the false label and intended serious harm. Negligent supervision would not satisfy this charge.
pushback: Juror 11: The message may support intent only after the prosecution proves Rina handled the tray. We cannot reverse that order.
Juror 11: Rina’s access creates opportunity, not a presumption that she used it criminally.
concede: Juror 11: I accept that the other three mismatches make the accidental explanation reasonable. Rina need prove no more.
final: Juror 11: The prosecution has not proved the deliberate act or intent beyond reasonable doubt. My verdict is not guilty.

jurors.label: Juror 12
jurors.persona: A patient listener who changes position when a timeline finally accounts for every event.

agree: Juror 12: The message came the night before, Rina moved ingredients at 11:35, and the printer failed at 11:42. Timing keeps the prosecution theory possible.
Juror 12: Orin’s tray was labelled between 11:43:02 and 11:43:49, inside the same window as three confirmed printer errors.
pushback: Juror 12: When did Rina leave the packing area? I want her movements placed beside the label timestamps.
Juror 12: The camera blind spot matters only if Rina was near it during the forty-seven-second printing window.
concede: Juror 12: Once all four trays are placed in order, the stale queue accounts for each label without requiring deliberate intervention.
final: Juror 12: The complete timeline supports an automatic labelling failure. The prosecution has not placed Rina at the tray when the label was applied.

gen_meta.reviewer: Codex Content Curator; independent QA rewrite and dynamics verification
